text: The surgeon approaches the pubic tubercle with precise, small movements, keeping his arms close together. He identifies the vessels and cauterizes them at the appropriate moments.
The surgeon has defatted the prostate within approximately six minutes, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon has taken the bladder down, revealing the defatted prostate. The robotic arms are positioned to facilitate precise movements during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia and takes down the puboprostatic ligaments, isolating the apex of the prostate. A DVC stitch is placed due to the tumor's location, followed by dissection through the anterior bladder neck. The surgeon performs nerve-sparing techniques while avoiding positive margins, leaving the apex dissection until full control is achieved.
The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia minimally to perform a retrograde nerve spread, focusing on a minimal apical dissection technique.
The surgeon manipulates the third arm of the robotic system to lift the prostate back and up, accentuating the hump of the small prostate after the anterior fat has been removed.
The surgeon performs lateral dissections before proceeding with anterior dissection, inspecting the bladder interior for ureters if necessary, especially in cases of a large bladder.
The surgeon uses electrocautery set to level two to perform a low dissection, carefully cutting through tissue with precise control.

The surgeon identifies circular muscle fibers during the dissection, indicating proximity to the prostate and seminal vesicles. The transition from round to circular fibers is visually noted as a predictable part of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies longitudinal fibers of the posterior bladder plate, indicating the location to proceed through. The seminal vesicles (SVs) are visible on the other side of these fibers.

The surgeon is at an anatomic junction, having previously cauterized the detrusor muscle, which visibly retracts upon cauterization. The surgeon now clips vessels to maintain a dry surgical field, avoiding the lateral plexus.
The surgeon takes down the lateral pedicles before reaching to grasp the ampulla, with the assistant skillfully identifying and utilizing a small anatomical window for access.
The surgeon uses cautery to dissect the ampulla away from the base of the seminal vesicle, then applies bipolar cautery to the medial vessel, ensuring precise dissection and vessel control.

The surgeon exposes both tips of the seminal vesicles, opening the surrounding space to increase prostate mobility. This technique facilitates easier dissection of the seminal vesicles, ensuring they are controlled before clipping the vas deferens.

The surgeon identifies the tip of the seminal vesicle before proceeding to take the ampulla, using clips to secure the area. In cases where the tissue is adherent, bipolar cautery may be used as an alternative to clips.

The surgeon performs an intrafascial dissection to release the nerve bundles from below, utilizing the XI system. The endopelvic fascia remains unopened initially, with plans to open it slightly later in the procedure.
The surgeon opens the tissue at the base of the prostate and searches for a specific anatomical point.
The surgeon observes the intact capsule with a thin fascial layer on top, preparing to place clips while leaving the lateral prostatic fascia undisturbed for a retrograde approach.

The surgeon carefully peels off tissue near the bladder neck, preserving the neurovascular bundle while performing a retrograde release. The dorsal venous complex (DVC) is released underneath without ligation.

The surgeon leaves the lateral prosthetic fascia intact and opens the endopelvic fascia using a unique approach. The retzius sparing technique involves accessing from underneath the plane and preserving the entire shelf of the dorsal venous complex (DVC).
The surgeon approaches the mid-prostate area, angling forward towards the urethra, while dissecting from the sides. The fascia appears more pink, indicating a thicker layer left intact.
The surgeon identifies the anterior tumor and observes the veins positioned superiorly. The apex of the prostate is visible, indicating the point to begin further dissection.
The surgeon adjusts the prostate margin, leaving a couple of millimeters of tissue on the prostate while preparing the surgical margin.
The surgeon stabilizes the urethra and dorsal vein, revealing a robust and thick circumferential urethra during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the area where a portion of the vein was intentionally left on one side to provide a cushion during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates the separation of neurovascular bundles from the prostatic fascia. The bundles remain attached to the lateral prostatic fascia, and the 'train tracks' appearance is no longer visible.

The surgeon performs a fish mouth closure of the bladder neck using a Bob suture, while maintaining a thermal approach. The posterior aspect is closed with a quill suture, and the veins are left unclamped as there is no significant bleeding.
The surgeon isolates and divides the posterior part of the urethra, clearly revealing the anatomy of the rhabdo sphincter. The remnants of the tissue are visible, ready to be joined.
The surgeon joins anatomical structures together, focusing on achieving hemostasis and creating a robust anastomosis.

The surgeon reconstructs the posterior plate by bringing together the cut edge of the denoviasis fascia, which was removed with the prostate. The second layer involves bringing the bladder down to the urethra, achieving hemostasis in the process.

The surgeon refines the posterior part of the anastomosis by reconstructing the first layer on the posterior aspect. The remnants of the bladder neck and posterior urethra are joined together, and additional stitches are passed to secure the anastomosis, aiming to reduce posterior leakage.
